screens.prompt: You already ran Welch's method on the burst (spectral-estimation module) and got a smooth, unremarkable-looking PSD — nothing that screamed "this is a clocked digital signal" rather than filtered noise. Given that the ordinary PSD is the α = 0 slice of a much richer object, does that smooth PSD mean the signal genuinely has no cyclostationary structure?
choices: Yes — if the PSD looks like plain noise, there's nothing more to find
No — the PSD only reports the α = 0 term; all the symbol-rate structure could be sitting at α ≠ 0, completely invisible to it
screens.reveal: No — and this is the reveal the whole case has been building to. The **spectral correlation function (SCF)**, $S_x^\alpha(f)$, is defined as the Fourier transform *over τ* of the cyclic autocorrelation function $R_x^\alpha(\tau)$ — the direct generalization of the Wiener–Khinchin PSD to every cycle frequency at once. The ordinary PSD is just $S_x^0(f)$, the $\alpha=0$ slice of the SCF. A modulated signal can have a PSD that looks exactly like filtered noise while its SCF lights up sharply at $\alpha = \pm 1/T_0$ and its harmonics — energy correlated across frequencies spaced $\alpha$ apart, a phenomenon Gardner called **spectral redundancy**. Background noise, lacking any clocked structure, generically has *no* such redundancy: its SCF is essentially flat away from $\alpha=0$. That asymmetry is precisely how a cyclostationary detector can find your burst where an energy detector or a plain PSD cannot.

screens.prompt: How does the ordinary power spectral density relate to the spectral correlation function $S_x^\alpha(f)$?

choices.text: The PSD is the α = 0 slice of the SCF: PSD(f) = S_x^0(f)
The PSD is the average of the SCF over all cycle frequencies α
choices.feedback: It isn't an average — it's specifically the single slice at α = 0. Averaging over α would mix in information the PSD structurally cannot see.

choices.text: The PSD and SCF are unrelated quantities computed from different data
choices.feedback: They come from the same underlying process and the same cyclic autocorrelation function — the SCF is a strict generalization of the PSD, not a separate, unrelated quantity.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — the PSD is one slice (α = 0) of the full two-dimensional SCF surface (frequency f and cycle frequency α). Every other slice is invisible to ordinary spectral analysis, which is exactly why your case needs the next module: actually estimating the SCF from real, finite, noisy data.
